exemplar.title: To what extent is sand dune formation in Mambrui, Kilifi County, affected by wind processes, acidity, human and environmental conditions?

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent is sand dune formation in Mambrui, Kilifi County, affected by wind processes, acidity, human and environmental conditions?

subject.title: Geography
subject.description:
**IB Geography** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **physical and human geography, environmental interactions, global challenges, and spatial patterns**, with a strong focus on **fieldwork, data analysis, case studies, and Geographic Information Systems (GIS)**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Geographic themes - structured questions | 2h 15min | 2h 15min | SL: 35% / HL: 35% |
| **Paper 2** | Geographic perspectives - data response and essays | 1h 15min | 1h 15min | SL: 40% / HL: 45% |
| **Paper 3** | Global interactions - extended essay responses | N/A | 1h | HL: 20% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Fieldwork investigation (2,500 words) | 20 hours | 20 hours | SL: 25% / HL: 20% |

strengths: Fieldwork question is explicitly geographical with clear theoretical linkage to Bagnoldâs aeolian transport theory.
Location is precisely identified (Mambrui, Kilifi County) with feasibility of a 500 m transect justified.
Clear hypotheses linked to syllabus concepts and primary data collection plan.
weaknesses: Question wording is lengthy and could be more concise to improve clarity.
Locational maps lack scale bars, full legends and precise grid references.
Hypotheses are split across pages; grouping them would strengthen cohesion.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strands reach or exceed the top-band descriptors: the question has clear theoretical grounding, the geographic context is detailed and feasible, and spatial representation is included. Minor lapses in mapping conventions do not detract from the overall excellence of this criterion.
feedback.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

strengths: Methods are described step-by-step with appropriate equipment lists (pH meter, anemometer, quadrats).
Sampling is systematic with replication and brief justifications to reduce bias.
Procedures are clearly linked to variables under investigation.
weaknesses: No formal risk assessment or health-and-safety considerations are included.
Calibration details (frequency of pH meter checks) and timing protocols for wind measurements are absent.
Quadrat dimensions and environmental controls (e.g., gust management) are not fully specified.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The methods are clearly described and relevant, and data-collection techniques are correctly used. However, omission of risk assessment, calibration protocols and some implementation details limit the work to the mid-band (2 marks).

strengths: Data collected are directly relevant and sufficient for preliminary analysis of all three variables.
Graphs (line, bar, scatter) and tables are appropriate and generally follow conventions.
Presentation methods (trendlines, ranked tables, Spearmanâs calculation) effectively convey patterns.
weaknesses: Occasional spelling and labeling errors (e.g., âDistnceâ on axes) and missing figure numbers/captions.
Error bars or measures of uncertainty are omitted from graphs.
Notation in the Spearman rank table is inconsistent (bold vs overline).
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Most data are pertinent and presented using suitable techniques, and only minor lapses in conventions occur. This places the work in the upper end of the 3â6 band, warranting 5 marks for strong overall treatment.
feedback.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

strengths: Descriptive and inferential statistics (Spearmanâs r) are correctly applied and interpreted.
Trends and anomalies are clearly linked to theory (Bagnoldâs transport, vegetation stabilization) and the research question.
Anomaly explanations (e.g., lime use at 400 m) are insightful and detailed.
weaknesses: Analysis lacks cross-variable synthesis that could deepen overall insight.
One interpretation paragraph is left incomplete and interrupts flow.
No confidence levels or discussion of statistical uncertainty are included.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The written analysis shows comprehensive linkage between patterns, theory and methods, with only minor gaps in synthesis and statistical depth. These strengths justify a high score of 7 out of 8.

strengths: Conclusion clearly restates findings for all three hypotheses and ties them back to dune stability.
All parts of the analysis are addressed and supported by evidence.
The conclusion is concise and directly follows from the data interpretation.
weaknesses: Final sentence is incomplete, interrupting coherence.
The research question could be explicitly restated to reinforce context.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion is fully supported by the analysis and meets the top-band descriptor. Minor editorial lapses do not detract from its overall clarity and completeness.

strengths: Evaluation outlines strengths and weaknesses with clear links to data reliability and field conditions.
Improvement suggestions (use of GIS/remote sensing, additional equipment) include explanations of how they would enhance accuracy and efficiency.
Discussion reflects on methodological limitations and their impact on results.
weaknesses: Some suggestions could be more technically detailed (e.g., laser rangefinders for inaccessible points).
The link between systematic sampling and improved data quality could be elaborated further.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation provides a comprehensive explanation of relevant strengths and weaknesses alongside actionable, well-justified improvements. This aligns with the highest band descriptors and merits the full 3 marks.

strandSelections.reasoning: Two locational maps and one annotated satellite image are included, fulfilling the requirement for spatial representation.  However, the hand-drawn base maps lack a scale bar and a clear legend, and north arrows are present but vague.  The satellite image shows the transect neatly but does not include a formal title or scale.  These lapses fit the descriptor of a map that is "included but with excess detail or lacking conventions", placing the work in band 2.

The fieldwork question is explicitly geographical, identifies three physical-human variables, and is linked to syllabus concepts such as aeolian processes and vegetation stabilisation.  The student also connects the question to Bagnoldâs theory in the introduction, demonstrating a theoretical basis that could generate testable hypotheses.  While the wording is rather long and could have been simplified, the essentials of clarity, focus and theoretical linkage are present, warranting the top band for this strand.

Line, bar and scatter graphs, ranked tables and a Spearman calculation are appropriate to the variables investigated.  Axis labels are provided and trendlines used, but some titles are misspelt and error bars or proportional symbols are absent.  Presentation is therefore appropriate though not the âmost effectiveâ, meriting the lower mark of the 3â4 band.

Techniques (quadrats, anemometer, pH probe) are appropriate and used correctly; replication (two trials, averaging) is noted.  Yet the dataset is only six points, acknowledged measurement errors (1-m ruler) reduce reliability, and no calibration or timing protocol for wind readings is provided.  This meets "techniques correctly used but with minimal data collection" rather than producing high-quality data, so band 2 is appropriate.

The student applies descriptive statistics and one inferential test (Spearmanâs r) for two variables, interpreting coefficient strength.  No confidence levels or further statistical tests are attempted, but the work exceeds purely descriptive treatment.  This fits band 3-4; the higher mark within the band is justified by correct computation and explanation of r.

Clear trends and anomalies are discussed for each graph with explicit linkage to the research question and supporting theories (Bagnold, vegetation stabilisation).  While linkage could be deeper, every pattern is related back to wind, pH or vegetation processes.  This satisfies the 5â6 band; the lower mark (5) is chosen because integration of theory and methods is competent but not exhaustive.

The location (Mambrui, Kilifi County, Kenya) is precisely identified with distances from Kilifi town, justification of accessibility, and clear reference to a 500-m coastal transect suitable for primary data collection.  The feasibility of collecting pH, wind-speed and vegetation data within three days is explicitly stated, exhibiting a strong geographic context and operational specificity.

Strengths and weaknesses are outlined with brief explanations (e.g., limited access causing distance estimations, shortage of tape measures).  The discussion relates points back to data reliability, moving beyond a simple list, satisfying the 2-mark band rather than the fully comprehensive top band.

Only six sampling points per variable yield a very small dataset and vegetation coverage is zero at four locations, limiting analytical depth.  While the data collected are relevant to the hypotheses, the quantity is borderline sufficient; thus the strand fits the upper end of the 1â2 band.

The conclusion restates findings for all three hypotheses and ties them to dune stability, directly supported by the presented analysis.  Although evaluative depth is modest, the requirement for a "clear conclusion fully supported" is met, fulfilling band 2.

Each hypothesis is analysed, but limitations of the small dataset, lack of measurement error discussion and absence of cross-variable synthesis produce noticeable gaps.  Consequently, the analysis is more than minimal but remains incomplete, fitting the 3-4 band at the lower mark.

Methods for all three variables are described step-by-step, include equipment lists and give brief justifications (e.g., systematic sampling to avoid bias, use of calibrated pH meter).  Nevertheless, explanations are not fully comprehensive: risk assessment, temporal controls and reference to theory beyond a sentence are absent.  The description is clear and relevant but not exhaustive, aligning with band 2.

Map and graph conventions (north arrow, figure numbers, axes) are used, yet scale bars are missing, units are inconsistent ("Distnce" spelling), and some figures lack captions.  These "occasional errors" place the work solidly in the 3â4 band; the lower mark is selected because the lapses are recurrent rather than rare.

Two practicable improvements (use of GIS/remote sensing, provision of additional tape measures) are given with a short explanation of how they would enhance accuracy and reduce time.  The suggestions are relevant but not highly detailed in terms of methodological impact, aligning with the 2-mark descriptor.

annotations.comment: The conclusion effectively ties hypotheses back to dune stability. For a stronger finish, link back explicitly to the original research question formulation.
annotations.groupName: Criterion E: Conclusion

annotations.comment: The fieldwork question demonstrates a clear theoretical basis by referencing Bagnoldâs aeolian transport theory, supporting the formation of testable hypotheses.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The question is overly long and complex, which may obscure focus. Condense wording to explicitly state variables and expected relationship for clarity.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The geographic context clearly specifies Mambrui and distances from Kilifi, showing feasibility of a 500 m transect for primary data collection within three days.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The context paragraph lacks precise coordinates or a grid reference for Mambrui. Include latitude/longitude to strengthen locational specificity.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Figure 1 includes a locational map but lacks a scale bar and clear legend. Add these conventions to improve spatial representation.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The link to the syllabus correctly identifies the optional B theme but could cite the exact syllabus section code (e.g., âOption B2â) for precision.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: Hypotheses 1 and 2 are clear, but Hypothesis 3 appears on the next page. For cohesion, list all three hypotheses together in one section.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The vegetation hypothesis is linked to stabilization theory but lacks a specific citation. Add a reference for âvegetation stabilisation theoryâ to support the claim.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Sampling strategy is well justified as systematic to avoid bias. Consider also controlling time of day for wind measurements to reduce variability.
annotations.groupName: Criterion B: Methods of Investigation

annotations.comment: Figure 2 correctly illustrates systematic sampling but lacks a figure number in the caption itself. Add âFigure 2:â to conform with conventions.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: The Spearman rank formula is correctly stated but could be formatted in LaTeX as $$r = 1 - \frac{6\sum d^2}{n^3 - n}$$ for clarity.
annotations.groupName: Criterion B: Methods of Investigation

annotations.comment: The pH measurement procedure is well structured with replication, but a risk assessment for handling soil and equipment is absent. Include health and safety notes.
annotations.groupName: Criterion B: Methods of Investigation

annotations.comment: Soil pH justification mentions calibration but lacks details on calibration frequency. State how often the pH meter was calibrated.
annotations.groupName: Criterion B: Methods of Investigation

annotations.comment: The anemometer procedure describes holding at 1.5 m, but fails to specify measurement duration. Define a fixed time (e.g., 60 s) for each wind reading.
annotations.groupName: Criterion B: Methods of Investigation

annotations.comment: Anemometer justifications note averaging but omit environmental controls (e.g., avoid gusts). Mention how gusty conditions were managed.
annotations.groupName: Criterion B: Methods of Investigation

annotations.comment: Vegetation quadrat procedure omits the size of the frame used. Specify frame dimensions (e.g., 0.5 mÂ²) to ensure comparability.
annotations.groupName: Criterion B: Methods of Investigation

annotations.comment: Justification for quadrat use correctly notes standardization, but could reference an ecological method source (e.g., Curtis & McIntosh, 1950).
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Soil pH graph axes are labelled, but the x-axis label contains a spelling error ("Distnce"). Correct for full adherence to conventions.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: No error bars are present on the soil pH graph. Including these would convey measurement uncertainty and strengthen data presentation.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Spearman rank table uses bold and overline inconsistently for ranks. Standardize notation (e.g., all ranks bold or all italics) for clarity.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Interpretation text ends abruptly ("As you move away from the 13"). Revise to complete the sentence and ensure coherence.
annotations.groupName: Criterion D: Written Analysis

annotations.comment: The anomaly at 400 m is well explained in terms of lime use. Consider supporting this with a photograph or field note extract.
annotations.groupName: Criterion D: Written Analysis

annotations.comment: The wind speed graph (Figure 7) appropriately shows trend but lacks a title that mentions variable names. Add a full title to enhance clarity.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Analysis links Bagnoldâs theory well to the wind data. To deepen interpretation, quantify obstacle density (e.g., vegetation % cover) at each point.
annotations.groupName: Criterion D: Written Analysis

annotations.comment: Vegetation graph axes seem clear, but error bars or boxplots could better illustrate distribution of quadrat estimates.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: The conclusion restates findings but leaves the last sentence incomplete. Finish the thought to produce a fully supported concluding paragraph.
annotations.groupName: Criterion E: Conclusion

annotations.comment: The evaluation lists valid strengths but does not explain how systematic sampling improved reliability. Detail the impact on data quality.
annotations.groupName: Criterion F: Evaluation

annotations.comment: The evaluation mentions access difficulties but does not propose a concrete alternative beyond GIS. Consider suggesting use of laser rangefinders for inaccessible points.
annotations.groupName: Criterion F: Evaluation

annotations.comment: Raw data tables in the appendix are well organized. Ensure units (e.g., msâ»Â¹ for wind speed) appear in table headers for completeness.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information
